blurb: A jewel-box rock-garden oregano grown for show, not the kitchen: wiry trailing stems drip with cone-like, overlapping bracts that shade from soft green to glowing rose-pink, with tiny pink flowers peeking through — like a cascade of ornamental hops.
water: Very low; xeric — sharp drainage essential, rots in wet soil
spread: Low trailing mat / spiller cascading over rocks, walls and container edges
seasons: Cone-like rose-pink hop bracts with pink flowers summer into fall; dries well for arrangements; foliage semi-evergreen but often dies back over a hard winter here
wildlife: An RHS 'plants for pollinators' selection — the small flowers draw bees and other pollinating insects
deer: Aromatic herbal foliage — deer and rabbits usually leave it alone
toxic: Non-toxic; in the culinary oregano genus, but grown as an ornamental — its foliage is milder and less pungent than kitchen oregano
origin: Garden hybrid of eastern-Mediterranean species (Turkey, Caucasus, Greece)
habitat: Wild parents grow on rocky limestone slopes and screes

care.hardiness: Marginal on the Front Range. It is rated about USDA zone 6–9 (RHS H4, hardy to roughly −10 to −5°C), so Boulder's 5b–6a winters sit right at its cold edge — it survives here only in a fast-draining, protected spot, and even then may die back in a severe winter and return in spring. The reliable way to keep it is in a gritty raised rock garden or trough, or grown in a container that is overwintered in a cold frame, unheated garage, or against a warm south wall; cold combined with wet winter clay is what kills it, not cold alone.[1][2][3]
care.planting: Set plants out in spring after the mid-May last-frost date into a gritty, sharply drained bed, raised rock garden, trough, gravel garden, or container — a south-facing, well-drained site is ideal. In the ground it needs excellent drainage to survive winter; grown in a pot (the safer option here), give it a lean, gritty mix and move it under cover for the coldest months, since container roots are far less cold-hardy than the top.[1][2][3]
care.sun: Full sun, preferably a warm south-facing exposure. Good light keeps the trailing stems tight and colors the bracts well; in shade the plant sprawls, blooms poorly, and is far more prone to rot.[1][2]
care.soil: Sharp, gritty drainage is the single most important requirement. It wants poor to moderately fertile, well-drained, preferably alkaline soil — chalk, sand, or gritty sandy loam — and performs best in lean, fast-draining ground. Heavy, wet Front-Range clay is the classic killer, so amend generously with grit or plant in a raised/trough situation.[1][2]
care.water: Very low. Once established it is genuinely xeric and drought-tolerant, wanting dry to at most medium moisture; water sparingly to settle it in, then let it dry between soakings. Overwatering — especially standing moisture in winter — causes crown and root rot.[1][3]
care.spacing: Give each plant about 10–12 in; the low trailing stems spread to roughly a foot wide and look best cascading over a rock, wall edge, or the rim of a pot rather than crowded.[1][3]
care.propagation: 'Kent Beauty' is a sterile-ish garden hybrid that will not come true from seed, so propagate it vegetatively — take basal or softwood cuttings in late spring, or lift and divide established clumps in spring.[1][2]
care.bloom: Through summer into early fall (roughly June–September) it carries its signature show: conspicuous, cone-like, overlapping deep rose-pink bracts arranged in drooping, hop-like whorls, with small tubular pink flowers emerging among them. The bracts hold and color up for weeks and dry well for arrangements.[1][2]
care.pruning: Minimal. Cut the old flowered stems back lightly in early spring as new growth begins, and tidy stray trailing stems as needed. Being woody-based, don't shear hard into bare, leafless old wood — like lavender and the sages it resprouts poorly from bald stems, so keep any cut above live foliage.[2]
care.feeding: Keep it lean — it thrives in poor to moderately fertile soil and needs little or no feeding. Rich soil or high-nitrogen fertilizer pushes soft, floppy growth that flops open and rots more easily; skip it.[1][2]
care.maintenance: Low-care apart from managing moisture. The whole job is keeping it dry-footed: ensure winter drainage, don't overwater, and in a container move it to a sheltered, unheated spot for winter. Leave the colored bracts for late-season interest or cut stems for drying.[1][3]
care.troubles: Crown and root rot from winter wet or poorly drained soil is by far the biggest threat — it is what fails most plants here. Otherwise it is largely pest- and disease-free, with only occasional aphids, spider mites, or fungal issues in humid, crowded, or overwatered conditions.[1][2]

edible.summary: Non-toxic and in the culinary oregano genus, but grown as an ornamental — the leaves are only mildly aromatic and it is not really used in cooking.[1]
edible.parts: The small aromatic leaves are edible like other Origanum, but this hybrid was bred for its bracts, not flavor: its foliage lacks the pungency and quality expected of a culinary oregano and is usually not used in the kitchen.[1]
edible.preparation: If you did use it, treat it like any oregano — a few fresh leaves as a mild seasoning or steeped for tea — but for actual cooking reach for true oregano (Origanum vulgare), which carries far more flavor.[1]
edible.caution: This is a garden hybrid grown for looks and is not documented as a culinary herb; there is no serious toxicity concern for people, but it is an ornamental, not a food crop, so enjoy it for the show rather than the plate.[1]

gaps: 'Kent Beauty' is a hybrid with limited open-licensed imagery: four shots are the true cultivar (Wikimedia Commons); the bract-mass and whole-plant habit shots are the near-identical parent species Origanum rotundifolium, captioned as such.
